text.title: Multi-Agent Basics Core Rules
text.summary: Seven testable rules for designing a multi-agent spec that survives contact with production.

rule.statement: Every agent role MUST have a single focused responsibility. If a role description contains the word "and", split it into two agents.
rule.rationale: Overlapping roles produce duplicated outputs, conflicting decisions, and wasted tokens; the orchestrator cannot tell which agent's answer is canonical.

rule.statement: Exactly one decision-making authority MUST be named for every conflict class — orchestrator, judge, majority vote, or human approver. Default = orchestrator.
rule.rationale: Without a named tie-breaker, conflicting agent outputs deadlock the synthesis step or get resolved by accident-of-order.

rule.statement: Every inter-agent message MUST be structured JSON conforming to a versioned schema (carry `schema_version` per `schema-version-pinning`). Free-form text passes between agents are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Free-form text introduces parsing fragility; versioned JSON makes drift a typed branch rather than a silent corruption.

rule.statement: Each handoff message MUST carry the full context the next agent needs — not just the delta. Agents have separate message histories; they cannot see each other's prior turns.
rule.rationale: Delta-only handoffs cause silent context loss; the downstream agent fills the gap with hallucinated assumptions.

rule.statement: The agent dependency graph MUST be a DAG. If A waits on B AND B waits on A, the run deadlocks. The orchestrator MUST refuse to start a run whose plan contains a cycle.
rule.rationale: Cycles surface as hangs in production; cycle detection is cheap at plan-time and free of false positives.

rule.statement: Every agent MUST have an explicit token budget AND wall-clock timeout. The orchestrator MUST emit an alert at 80% of either budget and hard-stop at 100%.
rule.rationale: Without per-agent caps, one stuck agent (loop, slow tool) drains the entire run budget; per-agent caps localize the failure.

rule.statement: The spec MUST declare an explicit termination condition: max turns, termination phrase, output-shape check, or DoD predicate. A multi-agent run with no termination is a bug.
rule.rationale: Open-ended runs converge to infinite loops or token-budget kill; explicit termination is what differentiates a pipeline from a chat.
